bdc-data-migration-004.easyUnderstanding: BDC testing simple 3-step process hai — jaise koi naya recipe try karne se pehle pehle ek chota batch banate ho, phir full quantity. Step 1: Chhota test karo — 5-10 records lo, kuch sahi data ke saath aur kuch galat data ke saath (missing mandatory field, wrong format, duplicate). MODE 'A' use karo taaki screens dikhti rahein aur tum visual check kar sako. Phir BDC log check karo SM35 ya application mein. Step 2: Volume test karo sandbox mein — complete file ke saath chalaao. Count compare karo: source mein 1000 records the, SAP mein 1000 hone chahiye. Step 3: Error handling path test karo — sirf happy path test karna beginner ki galti hai. Error wale records kya kar rahe hain? Log mein aa rahe hain? Functional team unhe dekh sakti hai? Phir MODE change karo: development mein MODE 'A' se shuru karo, UAT mein MODE 'E', production mein MODE 'N'. Simple rule: agar error records sahi se log ho rahe hain, aur count reconcile ho raha hai — testing done. Production mein kabhi bhi MODE 'A' mat chhodna.
bdc-data-migration-004.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Interview mein 'BDC testing approach kya hai' — yeh ek structured answer chahta hai jo real-world maturity dikhaye. Teen phases mein explain karo:

Phase 1 — Unit Testing (Developer Level): Pehla kaam hai SHDB recording verify karna. Development client mein single record test karo MODE 'A' se — screen by screen dekhte jao. Phir 5-10 records ka test set banao jo realistic ho: 3-4 valid records, 1-2 mandatory field missing records, 1 duplicate record, 1 invalid reference value (galat company code ya plant). Har error type ka response check karo — kya SAP sahi error message de raha hai, kya error log mein capture ho raha hai, kya valid records process ho rahe hain bina rukke. BDC log check karo: SM35 session method ke liye, ya apna custom error ALV Call Transaction ke liye.

Phase 2 — Volume Testing (Sandbox): Complete migration file ke saath sandbox mein full run karo. Count reconciliation critical hai: source file mein kitne records the — SAP mein utne hi hone chahiye minus genuine errors. Count mismatch matlab kuch silently fail hua — investigate karo. Performance bhi measure karo: 1000 records mein kitna time laga — multiply karo production volume ke saath. Cutover window mein fit hoga ya nahi?

Phase 3 — Error Path Testing (Most Important): Error handling path test karna mandatory hai — jo developers skip karte hain unka production mein bahut bura haal hota hai. Deliberately wrong records dalo aur check karo: (a) Error log mein aa raha hai? (b) Error message meaningful hai — functional team samjhegi? (c) Valid records ka processing nahi ruka? (d) Re-run facility kaam kar rahi hai — sirf failed records dobara chalaate ho toh duplicates nahi ban rahe?

MODE strategy: Development mein MODE 'A' — screens dekh ke verify karo field mapping sahi hai. UAT mein MODE 'E' — sirf errors pe screen dikhta hai, functional team review kare. Production mein mandatory MODE 'N' — fastest, no screen overhead. Final checklist before production: correct MODE, error log working, count reconciliation report ready, migration user authorization verified, rollback plan documented.
bdc-data-migration-004.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'My BDC testing approach is three phases. First, unit test with 5-10 records in MODE A — always include deliberate error records like missing mandatory fields and invalid values, not just happy-path data. I verify the error log captures failed records with meaningful messages and that valid records continue processing without stopping. Second, full volume test in sandbox with the complete file — count reconciliation is the acceptance criterion, any mismatch is investigated before proceeding. Third, and most critical, I specifically test the error handling path — I run a batch with 20% bad records to confirm error log, re-run capability, and no duplicate creation on retry. Finally, I test one complete cycle with the migration user credentials because developer authorization and migration user authorization are usually different, and authorization failures in production cutover are very costly.'

Hinglish tip: Interview mein sabse alag dikhna hai toh yeh bolo: 'Error records deliberately daalta hoon test mein — sirf happy path test karna kafi nahi hai.' Yeh line senior developer ki thinking dikhati hai. Aur add karo: 'Migration user se ek full test run karta hoon pehle — developer ke SAP_ALL se test karna aur migration user ke restricted rights — dono alag hain.' Interviewer ko samajh aata hai ki tum production-ready sochte ho.
bdc-data-migration-004.codeExamples:
" BDC Testing: Count reconciliation after migration run
DATA: lv_source_count TYPE i,
      lv_sap_count    TYPE i,
      lv_error_count  TYPE i.
" Source count from upload file
lv_source_count = lines( lt_input ).
" SAP count — records created in this run
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM kna1
  WHERE kunnr IN @lt_migrated_kunnr
  INTO @lv_sap_count.
" Error count from our error log
lv_error_count = lines( lt_errors ).
" Reconciliation report
WRITE: / 'Source records   :', lv_source_count.
WRITE: / 'Successfully loaded:', lv_sap_count.
WRITE: / 'Failed records    :', lv_error_count.
IF lv_source_count <> lv_sap_count + lv_error_count.
  WRITE: / 'WARNING: Count mismatch — investigate!'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: Why is testing error records more important than testing valid records in BDC?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Valid records toh kaam karne chahiye — yeh basic expectation hai. Real challenge hai: jab koi record fail hota hai toh kya hota hai? Kya valid records processing rukti hai? Kya error log mein sahi message aata hai? Kya functional team error dekh sakti hai? Kya re-run mein duplicate create nahi hota? Ye saare questions sirf error records test karne se answer hote hain. Happy-path-only testing ek false confidence deta hai — production mein pehla error aaya toh system crash ya silent failure.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Error record testing validates the defensive logic of the program — which is the part that protects production data. I test error cases to confirm: processing does not halt when one record fails, the error log captures the record key and SAP message for investigation, the functional team receives an actionable error report, and a re-run of failed records does not create duplicates. Happy-path testing only confirms the BDC mapping is correct — error testing confirms the program is production-safe.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project mein developer ne sirf valid records test kiye. Production mein pehla invalid record aaya — program mid-way mein dump hua. 800 records load hue, 4200 pending. Rollback? Partial data in system. 6 hours emergency fix. Simple error record test ne yeh bachaya hota.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Assuming error handling is working because 'I added IF sy-subrc <> 0' — you must actually test it with real error records to verify the error path executes correctly and the log is populated.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Test file design — always include error records
" Row 1: Valid customer — should succeed
" Row 2: Name1 blank — mandatory field missing
" Row 3: Duplicate KUNNR — already exists in system
" Row 4: Invalid BUKRS — company code not in system
" Row 5: Valid customer — should succeed
" After run: 2 success, 3 in error log — verify each message

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you do count reconciliation after a BDC migration run?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Count reconciliation simple math hai: source mein kitne records the, SAP mein kitne gaye, kitne fail hue — teen numbers hain, dono sides match karne chahiye. Source count = Success count + Error count. Agar yeh equation match nahi karta toh kuch silently fail hua — program ne records skip kar diye bina log kiye. Reconciliation ke baad SE16N ya application transaction se spot-check karo — 5-10 records manually verify karo ki fields sahi se fill hue hain, truncation nahi hua, special characters sahi hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Count reconciliation formula: Source records = Successfully created + Error records. Any deviation means records were silently dropped — which indicates a bug in error handling logic. After count validation, I do field-level spot-check on 5-10 migrated records in the application — verify name fields are not truncated, dates are in correct format, amounts match source. For financial data I do total-amount reconciliation: sum of migrated amounts must equal sum from source file to the last decimal.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Vendor master migration: source file 2500 records. Program reported 2300 success, 150 errors. 2300 + 150 = 2450, not 2500. 50 records missing. Investigation: a SPLIT statement was failing on special character in vendor name — those records were silently skipped. Fixed by adding CATCH for string operation exceptions.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Only checking success count without verifying source total — if your error log is also buggy, you may have both incorrect success count and incorrect error count, both masking silent data loss.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Reconciliation check with assertion
lv_total_processed = lv_success_count + lv_error_count.
IF lv_total_processed <> lv_source_count.
  MESSAGE |Reconciliation FAIL: Source { lv_source_count }, | &
          |Processed { lv_total_processed }. | &
          |Missing { lv_source_count - lv_total_processed } records!|
    TYPE 'W'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the correct MODE sequence for BDC development to production?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: MODE ek traffic light ki tarah hai. Development mein MODE 'A': sari screens dikhti hain — tum visually check karte ho ki sahi screen aa raha hai, sahi field fill ho raha hai. Yeh slow hai but development ke liye perfect. UAT mein MODE 'E': sirf error pe screen dikhti hai — functional team interactive mode mein error reason dekh sakti hai aur decide kar sakti hai. Production mein MODE 'N': koi screen nahi — fastest, background mein chalti hai, no dialog overhead. Galti: developer 'A' mein test karta hai but 'N' mein nahi — 'N' mein kuch fields behave alag kar sakte hain jaise popups jo 'A' mein visible the.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The MODE progression is: MODE A during development for visual screen-by-screen verification of field mapping, MODE E during UAT so functional users can review errors interactively, and MODE N for production — mandatory for background execution with no screen overhead. Critical: always run at least one test cycle in MODE N before production because some BDC programs behave differently in MODE N — popups that appear in MODE A are silently skipped in MODE N, causing the transaction to fail with a different error signature.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Development testing fine in MODE 'A'. Switched to 'N' for performance test — all records failed. Root cause: a confirmation popup appeared in the transaction that MODE 'A' showed on screen (developer pressed Enter), but MODE 'N' needed an explicit BDC_OKCODE entry to handle it. Added BDC entry for popup dismissal — fixed. Always test in MODE 'N' before production.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Testing only in MODE A and assuming MODE N will behave identically — popup handling is a common difference that only surfaces when you actually test in MODE N.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Mode constants for clarity
CONSTANTS: c_mode_visible TYPE c VALUE 'A',  " Development
           c_mode_error   TYPE c VALUE 'E',  " UAT
           c_mode_batch   TYPE c VALUE 'N'.  " Production
" Use parameter on selection screen to control mode
PARAMETERS: p_mode TYPE c DEFAULT 'N'.
CALL TRANSACTION 'XD01' USING lt_bdc
  MODE p_mode MESSAGES INTO DATA(lt_msg).
" Before production: verify p_mode defaults to 'N'

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you test BDC performance before production cutover?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Performance test karna matlab: production volume ka estimate nikalana pehle. Simple calculation: 100 records test karo aur time measure karo. Phir extrapolate karo — agar 100 records mein 5 minutes lage toh 10,000 records mein 500 minutes yaani 8+ hours. Cutover window sirf Saturday night 8 PM to Sunday morning 6 AM hai — 10 hours. 8 hours BDC ke liye, 2 hours validation ke liye — tight but doable. Agar fit nahi hota toh optimize karo: MODE 'N' already hai na? Package size sahi hai? Parallel jobs se split kar sakte ho?
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Performance testing approach: run exactly 100 records with production-equivalent data in sandbox, measure clock time precisely, then extrapolate to full production volume. If the estimate exceeds the cutover window, optimize: ensure MODE N is set, increase package size if using session method, consider splitting load into parallel background jobs via SM36. Document the estimated runtime in the cutover plan so stakeholders have realistic expectations. Always add 20% buffer for infrastructure variance.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Material master migration: 50,000 records. Tested 500 records — 4 minutes. Extrapolated: 400 minutes = 6.7 hours. Cutover window: 8 hours. Added 20% buffer: 8 hours. Exactly at limit. Split into 2 parallel jobs: 3.5 hours each. Comfortable fit with validation time remaining.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not doing performance testing before cutover and discovering during the actual go-live weekend that the migration will take 20 hours in an 8-hour window — no options left at that point.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Performance measurement wrapper
DATA: lv_start TYPE timestampl,
      lv_end   TYPE timestampl,
      lv_secs  TYPE p DECIMALS 2.
GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_start.
" ... run 100 records ...
GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_end.
lv_secs = lv_end - lv_start.
WRITE: / |100 records: { lv_secs } seconds|.
WRITE: / |Estimated for 10000 records: { lv_secs * 100 / 3600 } hours|.

followupAnswerBank.question: What should you verify in SM35 after running a BDC session method test?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: SM35 session method ka control center hai. Test ke baad SM35 mein jaao aur check karo: session status kya hai — Processed, Error, ya New? Processed records count correct hai? Error records hai — inka detail dekho (session double-click karo, error records expand karo). Error message meaningful hai? Jo records fail hue unka data SM35 mein visible hai? Agar session 'Error' status mein hai toh reprocess option available hai — test karo ki reprocess kaam karta hai ya nahi. SM35 mein log download facility hai — functional team ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: After a session method BDC test, in SM35 I verify: session status shows Processed, record count matches expected, error sessions show meaningful SAP messages for each failed record, error records retain original data for reprocessing, and the session log is downloadable for functional team review. I also test the reprocess capability — run the error session again and verify it creates records successfully without duplicates, confirming the error handling is re-entrant.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: SM35 session review process: click on session name, expand error records, check each message. Common finding: session shows 'error' but error message just says 'Screen sequence error' — means a new popup appeared that BDC recording did not capture. Fix: re-record SHDB with that popup included.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Treating a session with 'Processed' status as fully successful — a session can show Processed with some individual records in error state. Always check the detailed error count inside the session.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Session Method: check SM35 post-test programmatically
" Read session statistics from APQD table
SELECT SINGLE qid, status, endsess, errses
  FROM apqd
  WHERE groupid = 'ZMIG_TEST'
  INTO @DATA(ls_session).
WRITE: / 'Status :', ls_session-status.
WRITE: / 'End count:', ls_session-endsess.
WRITE: / 'Errors   :', ls_session-errses.
" 0 errors = clean test. Any errors = investigate SM35 detail.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you test BDC re-run capability to ensure no duplicate records?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Re-run test critical hai — migration mein fail records dobara run karne pad sakte hain. Danger: agar duplicate check nahi hai toh customer already create hua toh dobara create hone ki koshish hogi — error aayega ya worse, duplicate bana. Test karo: pehle 10 records run karo successfully. Phir same 10 records dobara run karo — expected behavior: SAP 'already exists' error de aur gracefully skip kare ya update mode mein switch kare. Yeh ensure karta hai ki re-run safe hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Re-run testing verifies the program is idempotent — safe to run multiple times without creating duplicates or corrupting data. I run 10 records successfully, then deliberately re-run the same 10 records. Expected: duplicate records trigger 'already exists' error which is captured in the error log, not a silent overwrite or program dump. For objects that support update mode, I verify the update path also works correctly. This test is mandatory before production — cutover runs often need partial re-runs after network or system interruptions.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Vendor master BDC: re-run test revealed that duplicate vendor creation triggered a popup asking 'Create anyway?' — in MODE 'N' this popup was silently handled as 'No', causing a sy-subrc error. Error was being logged as duplicate. Correct behavior — confirmed safe. If popup had been silently confirmed as 'Yes', we would have had duplicate vendors in production.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not testing re-run at all — discovering during production that a network interruption mid-run requires a re-run, and the re-run creates 3000 duplicate records, is a catastrophic situation.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Duplicate guard before BDC call
SELECT SINGLE kunnr FROM kna1
  WHERE kunnr = @ls_input-kunnr
  INTO @DATA(lv_exists).
IF sy-subrc = 0.  " Already exists
  ls_error-kunnr  = ls_input-kunnr.
  ls_error-msgtxt = 'SKIP: Customer already exists in SAP'.
  APPEND ls_error TO lt_errors.
  CONTINUE.  " Do not attempt BDC create
ENDIF.
" Only run BDC if record does not exist

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you involve the functional team in BDC migration testing?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC testing sirf developer ka kaam nahi hai — functional team ka role equally important hai. Developer kaam karta hai: program likhna, technical test karna, error log banana. Functional team kaam karti hai: test data provide karna jo realistic ho, migrated records verify karna application mein (jaise XD03 pe customer kholo aur fields check karo), error log se errors samajhna aur source data fix karna, sign-off dena ki data sahi hai. Developer data sahi hai ya nahi yeh nahi jaanta — functional team jaanti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Functional team involvement in BDC testing: they provide realistic test data including edge cases from legacy system, verify migrated records in SAP application transactions — not just SE16N table level but actual business transactions like XD03 for customer, MM03 for material. They review the error log for meaningful messages and confirm they can take corrective action. Finally they provide formal sign-off that migrated data is business-correct before production. I present an ALV error report specifically designed for functional team review — not raw technical dump.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Functional sign-off process: developer runs migration in QA, provides count report + error report. Functional lead verifies 30 random records in XD03, checks critical fields (payment terms, account group, tax classification). Finds 2 field mapping errors in BDC — fixed. Re-run. Functional signs off. This process caught business-logic errors that unit testing would never have found.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Showing functional team a SE16N table dump and asking 'does this look correct?' — they cannot evaluate raw table data. Show them in the actual SAP application transaction they use daily.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Functional-friendly error report (ALV)
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_err_report,
  kunnr   TYPE kna1-kunnr,
  name1   TYPE kna1-name1,
  error   TYPE string,
  action  TYPE string,  " 'Fix source data and resubmit'
END OF ty_err_report.
" Use cl_salv_table for clean ALV display
" Functional team downloads to Excel, fixes, sends back

followupAnswerBank.question: What is mock cutover and why is it important before go-live?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Mock cutover matlab go-live ka full rehearsal karna — same steps, same timeline, same people, but in sandbox ya QA system. Jaise theatre mein dress rehearsal hoti hai show se pehle. Mock cutover mein discover karte ho: kaunse steps kitna time lete hain, kahan dependencies hain (vendor master load ke baad hi customer-vendor link create hoga), authorization issues, network issues, unexpected errors. Minimum 2 mock runs karo before actual go-live — pehle run mein issues milenge, doosre run mein fixes verify hogi, teesre run mein timing confirm hogi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Mock cutover is a full rehearsal of the production migration with the same sequence of jobs, same people, same data volumes — executed in sandbox or QA system. It validates end-to-end timing (will we finish within the cutover window?), job sequencing dependencies, authorization setup for migration users, and identifies any last-minute issues. Industry standard is minimum two mock runs: first to discover issues, second to validate fixes. I document actual timings from the mock to create a realistic cutover plan with timestamps for each job.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Our first mock cutover: planned 10 hours, actual 16 hours. Root causes: material master BAPI was slower than BDC test had indicated, and open FI items had 3x more records than estimated due to a data extraction error. Second mock: 11 hours with optimizations. Actual go-live: 10.5 hours. Without mocks, we would have failed the go-live window.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Skipping mock cutover because 'we tested everything in QA already' — testing individual components is not the same as testing the full end-to-end sequence under time pressure with all dependencies.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Mock cutover run tracking table
DATA: BEGIN OF ls_cutover_log,
  job_name   TYPE string,
  start_time TYPE timestampl,
  end_time   TYPE timestampl,
  rec_count  TYPE i,
  error_cnt  TYPE i,
  status     TYPE string,
END OF ls_cutover_log.
" Log each job: start time, end time, counts
" Compare mock run 1 vs mock run 2 vs actual go-live

bdc-data-migration-005.easyUnderstanding: Interview mein BDC ke kuch trap questions hote hain jo simple lagte hain but wrong answer dene pe interviewer immediately samajhta hai ki candidate real experience nahi rakhta. Sabse common trap: 'BDC kisi bhi transaction ke liye kaam karta hai na?' — Wrong answer: 'Haan bilkul.' Sahi answer: 'Nahi — complex ALV-based transactions, popup-heavy transactions, ya JavaScript-based Fiori screens ke liye BDC kaam nahi karta ya bahut unreliable hota hai.' Doosra trap: 'Session method ya Call Transaction — large volume ke liye kaunsa better hai?' — Wrong answer: 'Call Transaction kyunki simple hai.' Sahi answer: 'Session Method — background mein chalta hai, errors gracefully handle karta hai, SM35 mein audit trail milta hai.' Teesra trap: 'BDC recording transport ke baad break kyo ho jaati hai?' — Wrong answer: 'Transport se kuch nahi hota.' Sahi answer: 'Target system mein screen sequence different ho sakti hai — re-record karna padta hai target system mein.' Ye teen trap answers jaanlo — interviewer impressed hoga ki tum practically jaante ho, theory se zyada.
bdc-data-migration-005.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Interview trap questions ka purpose: interviewer dekhna chahta hai ki candidate sirf textbook answers deta hai ya real-world experience hai. BDC mein top 6 trap questions aur unke analysis:

Trap 1 — 'BDC kisi bhi SAP transaction ke liye kaam karta hai?': Confident 'No' answer do. Explain karo: BDC screen-based simulation karta hai — traditional dynpro screens ke liye perfect. Lekin kuch transactions kaam nahi karte: (a) ALV-based transactions jahan user grid cell pe directly type karta hai — BDC field-level simulation nahi kar sakta. (b) Pop-up heavy transactions jo dynamic popups generate karte hain based on data — recording mein woh popup nahi tha. (c) Fiori/Web Dynpro transactions — completely different technology, BDC ABAP DYNPRO simulate karta hai. (d) Transactions jo third-party ActiveX controls use karte hain. Conclusion: BDC standard SAP dynpro transactions ke liye reliable hai, modern UI/ALV-heavy ke liye BAPI ya direct API use karo.

Trap 2 — 'Session Method vs Call Transaction — large volume ke liye?': Session Method answer hai. Reason: Session method background mein run hota hai (SM35 process karta hai), errors per-record log hote hain automatically, partial failures poori job nahi rokti, audit trail complete hoti hai. Call Transaction synchronous hai — large volume mein dialog time, commit per record, aur error handling developer pe depend karta hai.

Trap 3 — 'BDC recording transport ke baad break kyo hoti hai?': Screen sequence system-specific hoti hai. DEV system mein jo screens aate hain, QA ya PROD mein same screens same order mein nahi aate — system configuration, user settings, organizational assignments alag ho sakti hain. Isliye: BDC programs transport karo, but target system mein recording verify karo pehle. Agar break hua toh SHDB se re-record karo target system mein.

Trap 4 — 'BDC vs BAPI — kab kya use karo?': Simple rule: BAPI available hai toh BAPI. BAPI nahi hai toh BDC. But nuance yeh jaanna chahiye: BAPI faster, stable, screen-independent, S/4HANA cloud compatible. BDC flexible — koi bhi standard dynpro transaction ke liye kaam karta hai. Z-transactions ke liye BDC hi option hai.

Trap 5 — 'Ek BDC session mein kitne records hone chahiye?': No fixed number, but practical limit: too many records ek session mein = SM35 performance issue. Recommended: 1000-5000 records per session depending on transaction complexity. Large migration mein multiple sessions banao.

Trap 6 — 'BDC se data rollback kaise karte ho?': Trick: BDC khud rollback nahi karta easily. CALL TRANSACTION auto-commits — rollback nahi hota standard way se. Rollback ke liye: (a) Session Method mein session process karne se pehle discard karo. (b) Application-level reversal transactions use karo. (c) Archiving. Prevention better than cure — validate pehle, load baad mein.
bdc-data-migration-005.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'Common BDC trap questions I watch out for: First, BDC does not work for all transactions — complex ALV-grid transactions, Fiori UI, and popup-heavy transactions are not reliably handled by BDC — BAPI or direct API is the correct approach there. Second, for large volume the Session Method is superior to Call Transaction — it runs in background via SM35, handles errors per record without stopping the full run, and provides a complete audit log. Third, BDC recordings can break after transport to a target system because screen sequences can differ by system configuration — always verify the recording in the target system after transport and re-record in SHDB if needed. These three points separate candidates with real BDC experience from those who only read documentation.'

Hinglish tip: Trap question ka sahi jawab dene ke baad ek real incident add karo: 'Ek project mein transport ke baad BDC break hua — target system mein ek extra confirmation screen tha jo DEV mein nahi tha. SHDB se re-record kiya target system mein aur fixed.' Yeh specificity dikhati hai real experience — interviewer immediately confident ho jaata hai.
bdc-data-migration-005.codeExamples:
" Trap: BDC with unexpected popup — handle it explicitly
" Without popup handling: BDC fails silently in MODE 'N'
" With popup handling: explicitly dismiss confirmation popup
ls_bdc-program  = 'SAPLSPO1'.   " Popup program
ls_bdc-dynpro   = '0100'.        " Popup screen
ls_bdc-dynbegin = 'X'.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'SPOP-OPTION1'.   " First option (usually Yes/Ja)
ls_bdc-fval = 'J'.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'BDC_OKCODE'.
ls_bdc-fval = 'OPTION1'.
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
" This popup handling prevents silent failure in MODE 'N'
" Always check SHDB recording includes all popups

followupAnswerBank.question: Can BDC handle ALV grid-based SAP transactions?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Yeh ek classic trap hai. ALV grid traditional dynpro field mapping se completely alag kaam karta hai. BDC field name se value fill karta hai — ALV grid mein cells ka traditional field name structure nahi hota. Kuch simple ALV screens ke liye workarounds hain — grid column pe focus karo, but unreliable hai. Best answer: ALV-based transactions ke liye BDC avoid karo. BAPI use karo, ya agar available nahi toh LSMW/Direct Input check karo. Custom Z-ALV transactions ke liye alternative design consider karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC cannot reliably handle ALV grid-based transactions because ALV grids do not expose traditional DYNPRO field names that BDC can map to. BDC works with classical DYNPRO screen fields — ALV uses a control framework where grid cells are managed differently. For SAP standard transactions that use ALV grids, the correct approach is to use the corresponding BAPI or Direct Input program. This is a key limitation candidates should know — interviewers test this to identify candidates who assume BDC is universal.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project tried to BDC a transaction with ALV line items — SHDB recording produced field names like 'SAPLALDB-DUMMY' with no meaningful mapping. Spent 2 days troubleshooting. Solution: found BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE which handled the same transaction cleanly in 2 hours. Time wasted on wrong approach.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Attempting to BDC ALV-based transactions and spending days trying workarounds — always evaluate whether the transaction uses classical DYNPRO or ALV before starting BDC development.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" For ALV-based transactions: use BAPI instead of BDC
" Example: Goods Movement (MIGO uses ALV) — use BAPI
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_GOODSMVT_CREATE'
  EXPORTING goodsmvt_header = ls_header
  TABLES    goodsmvt_item   = lt_items
            return          = lt_return.
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT' EXPORTING wait = 'X'.
" Clean, reliable, no ALV screen issues

followupAnswerBank.question: What happens to BDC if a new mandatory field is added in SAP after go-live?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Yeh ek maintenance trap hai. SAP support pack ya configuration change ke baad agar transaction mein naya mandatory field add hua toh existing BDC program fail hoga — kyunki naye field ka BDC entry nahi hai. Symptoms: BDC runs with no ABAP dump but all records fail with 'field required' error. Fix: SHDB mein transaction dobara record karo, naya field identify karo, BDC program mein entry add karo, test karo. Agar migration already complete ho gayi toh yeh issue nahi aata — but agar BDC program ongoing upload ke liye use ho raha hai toh maintenance issue hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: If a new mandatory field is added to a SAP transaction after BDC program development, the program will fail for all records because the new field has no BDC entry — SAP will not save the record without the required field. The fix requires re-recording SHDB to identify the new field, adding the BDC entry for that field, testing, and transporting. This is a maintenance cost of BDC — BAPI-based programs are more resilient because BAPIs have defined interfaces that SAP versions separately.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: After EHP upgrade: customer master BDC started failing for all records. Root cause: new 'Industry Code' field made mandatory in XD01. SHDB re-record identified the field. Added one BDC entry, provided default value 'GENERAL', all records started passing. 2 hours fix — but happened unexpectedly in production ongoing upload program.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not having a regression test suite for BDC programs used as ongoing upload tools — support packages can break them at any time. Monthly regression run is a best practice.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Adding new mandatory field to existing BDC
" Old SHDB recording did not include this field
" After re-record in updated system:
ls_bdc-fnam = 'KNA1-BRSCH'.   " New industry code field
ls_bdc-fval = 'GENERAL'.      " Default value agreed with functional
APPEND ls_bdc TO lt_bdc. CLEAR ls_bdc.
" Insert at correct position in screen sequence

followupAnswerBank.question: Why might a BDC program work in DEV but fail in QA or PROD?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: DEV-QA-PROD differences ek common trap topic hai. Reasons BDC DEV mein kaam karta hai QA/PROD mein nahi: (1) Screen sequence: DEV mein kuch configuration already set hai (company code, plant) — QA mein nahi, different screens aate hain. (2) User-specific settings: developer ka SU3 settings (date format, decimal notation) recording ko affect karte hain — migration user ka alag ho sakta hai. (3) Authorization: developer SAP_ALL, migration user restricted. (4) Data dependencies: DEV mein vendor X exist karta tha, QA mein nahi — foreign key validation fail. (5) Number ranges: DEV mein internal number range available, QA mein exhausted.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC programs can fail in QA or PROD but work in DEV due to: different screen sequences caused by varying system configuration (organizational structure, mandatory field settings); user-specific settings like date format or decimal notation affecting field values; authorization differences between developer and migration user IDs; data dependencies such as reference master data that exists in DEV but not QA; or number ranges exhausted in QA. Always test in QA with the exact migration user ID and production-representative data before cutover.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: DEV mein BDC perfect, QA mein fail. Root cause: DEV mein user settings mein date format DD.MM.YYYY tha, QA mein MM/DD/YYYY. BDC date field hardcoded '01.01.2024' — QA ne galat format samjha. Fix: date always convert to internal SAP format (YYYYMMDD) in ABAP before BDC fill.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Transporting BDC programs to production without testing in QA system — assuming if it works in DEV it will work everywhere. Always have a QA validation step in the transport process.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Always convert date to internal format for BDC — system-independent
DATA: lv_date_internal TYPE d.
" Convert from any external format to internal YYYYMMDD
CALL FUNCTION 'CONVERT_DATE_TO_INTERNAL'
  EXPORTING date_external        = ls_input-date_str
  IMPORTING date_internal        = lv_date_internal
  EXCEPTIONS date_external_is_invalid = 1.
ls_bdc-fnam = 'BSEG-BUDAT'.
ls_bdc-fval = lv_date_internal.  " Safe across all systems

followupAnswerBank.question: Interviewer asks: 'Which is better — BDC or BAPI?' How do you answer this trap?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Yeh ek loaded question hai — koi absolute answer nahi hai. Wrong answers: 'BAPI always better' ya 'BDC always better'. Sahi answer: 'Depends on the scenario.' BAPI better hai jab: standard SAP object ke liye C1-released BAPI available hai, large volume mein performance critical hai, S/4HANA ya cloud project hai, stability aur maintainability priority hai. BDC better hai jab: transaction ke liye BAPI available nahi hai, custom Z-transaction hai, screen-level validation important hai (BDC triggers same validations as manual entry), quick migration without deep BAPI setup needed.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Neither is universally better — the correct choice depends on availability and requirements. I prefer BAPI when a C1-released BAPI exists for the object: it is faster, screen-independent, S/4HANA cloud compatible, and more stable across upgrades. I use BDC when no BAPI exists, for custom Z-transactions, or when screen-level business validations must be triggered identically to manual user entry. The practical decision: check for BAPI first in SE37, if found use it, if not fall back to BDC or investigate LSMW.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project decision matrix: customer master — BAPI_CUSTOMER_CREATEFROMDATA1 exists, use BAPI. Custom purchase requisition Z-transaction — no BAPI, use BDC. Vendor master — BAPI_VENDOR_CREATEFROMDATA exists, use BAPI. Custom goods movement Z-screen — no BAPI, BDC. This hybrid approach is standard in real migration projects.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Defaulting to BDC for everything because it is more familiar — losing BAPI benefits like speed, stability, and S/4HANA compatibility. Or defaulting to BAPI for everything and realizing halfway through that no BAPI exists for the required transaction.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Decision logic: BAPI first, BDC fallback
" Step 1: Check SE37 — does a C1-released BAPI exist?
" Step 2: If yes — implement BAPI
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_CUSTOMER_CREATEFROMDATA1' ...  " Preferred
" Step 3: If no BAPI — implement BDC
CALL TRANSACTION 'XD01' USING lt_bdc MODE 'N' ...  " Fallback
" Document decision reason in technical spec

followupAnswerBank.question: Trap: 'What do you do if BDC session method errors out completely — not just individual records?'
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Yeh system-level failure ka trap hai — individual record error nahi, poori session fail. Reasons: job running SM35 crashes, database connectivity issue, application server overload, memory overflow. SM35 mein session status 'Interrupted' ya 'Error' dikhega. Steps: SM35 mein jaao, session select karo, 'Log' dekho — system-level error message milega. Agar aborted hai partially: jo records process ho gaye confirm karo (session error log mein processed count milega), restart karo session from the point of failure if possible, ya naye session mein remaining records chalaao.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A complete session method failure — where SM35 shows session as Interrupted or Aborted — is usually a system-level issue not a data issue. Steps: check SM35 log for the system error, check SM21 system log for database or memory errors, identify how many records were processed before failure using the session log. For the remaining records, create a new BDC session with only the unprocessed records — this requires the migration program to support checkpoint-based restart. Always design session method migrations with a restart log table that tracks which records have been successfully processed.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Production cutover: session method job aborted at 3 AM — database tablespace full. DBA fixed tablespace in 30 minutes. Because we had a restart table (ZTMIG_PROCESSED with kunnr + status), we restarted from record 3200 instead of record 1. Completed remaining 1800 records in 20 minutes. Without restart table: would have had to rerun all 5000 with duplicate handling complexity.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not building a restart/checkpoint mechanism in session method migrations — assuming the run will always complete cleanly. Infrastructure failures happen — always design for restart.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Checkpoint restart table design
TABLES: ztmig_checkpoint.
" Before processing each record:
SELECT SINGLE * FROM ztmig_checkpoint
  WHERE run_id = @p_runid AND rec_key = @ls_input-kunnr
  INTO @DATA(ls_cp).
IF sy-subrc = 0 AND ls_cp-status = 'S'. CONTINUE. ENDIF. " Already done
" After successful processing:
MODIFY ztmig_checkpoint FROM VALUE #(
  run_id = p_runid  rec_key = ls_input-kunnr  status = 'S' ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you answer: 'BDC is outdated — why would you use it in a new project?'
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Yeh ek opinion trap hai — interviewer dekhna chahta hai ki tum defensive ho jaate ho ya balanced answer dete ho. BDC outdated hai in some contexts but still relevant hai: kisi bhi traditional SAP dynpro transaction ke liye jo BAPI ya modern API provide nahi karta, BDC sahi choice hai. Z-transactions ke liye BAPI banane ki effort zyada hoti hai — BDC faster hai. On-premise S/4HANA mein BDC still supported hai. New project mein BDC architecture decision: pehle check karo BAPI/OData available hai ya nahi. Agar hai — use karo. Nahi hai — BDC practical choice hai. Hybrid approach recommend karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC is not outdated — it is the appropriate tool for specific scenarios in on-premise SAP systems. For any standard SAP dynpro transaction where no BAPI or modern API exists, BDC is the correct choice. Custom Z-transactions have no BAPI by definition — BDC is often the most practical option. For S/4HANA on-premise, BDC remains fully supported. The modern best practice is API-first — check for BAPI or OData API first, use them when available, and use BDC as the fallback. I would not use BDC in S/4HANA Public Cloud or for objects where stable APIs exist.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: New S/4HANA on-premise project: 15 migration objects. 10 had BAPIs — used BAPI for those. 5 custom Z-transactions — no BAPI possible, used BDC. Hybrid approach was right answer. Presenting this as 'we evaluated each object and chose appropriate tool' shows maturity to interviewer.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Defending BDC for everything OR dismissing BDC completely — both are wrong. The correct answer demonstrates you can evaluate tools contextually based on availability, volume, and project constraints.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Modern migration approach: API-first with BDC fallback
" Object 1: Customer master — BAPI available
"   → BAPI_CUSTOMER_CREATEFROMDATA1
" Object 2: Material master — BAPI available
"   → BAPI_MATERIAL_SAVEDATA
" Object 3: Custom Z pricing table — no BAPI
"   → BDC for Z-transaction ZPR01
" Object 4: S/4HANA standard FI posting — BAPI
"   → BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the trap with BDC and special characters or Unicode data?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Special characters BDC mein ek silent killer hai. Problem: BDC FVAL ek string field hai — agar data mein umlaut (ä, ö, ü), Arabic, Chinese ya special symbols hain toh encoding issue ho sakta hai. ABAP Unicode system mein usually fine hota hai — but file reading mein issue ho sakti hai. CSV file ASCII mein save ki, UTF-8 characters the — SPLIT ne galat split kiya kyunki CSV delimiter comma tha aur string mein comma bhi tha (address field mein 'Mumbai, Maharashtra'). GUI_UPLOAD mein codepage parameter sahi set karo. BDC FVAL pe string truncation: BDC field value 132 characters se lamba nahi hona chahiye — truncation silent hoti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Special characters cause two BDC issues. First, encoding: always read upload files with the correct codepage parameter in GUI_UPLOAD to handle UTF-8, Unicode, or regional character sets. Second, delimiter collision: if address or name fields contain commas and the file is comma-delimited, SPLIT AT ',' produces wrong results — use tab-delimited files or quoted CSV with proper parsing. Third, field value length: BDC FVAL should not exceed 132 characters — longer values are silently truncated causing data corruption. Always validate field lengths before BDC fill.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Customer name migration: 'Müller & Söhne GmbH' — German umlaut. GUI_UPLOAD without codepage parameter read garbled characters. Field in SAP: 'M?ller'. Customer complained post-migration. Fix: added codepage = '4110' (UTF-8) to GUI_UPLOAD. Re-migrated affected records. 45-minute fix that could have been prevented by testing with non-ASCII data.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Testing BDC only with pure ASCII test data and discovering Unicode or special character issues only during actual migration when real legacy data with regional characters is loaded.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" GUI_UPLOAD with correct codepage for special characters
CALL FUNCTION 'GUI_UPLOAD'
  EXPORTING filename  = p_file
             filetype  = 'ASC'
             codepage  = '4110'  " UTF-8 — handles Unicode, umlauts
  TABLES    data_tab  = lt_raw.
" Also validate field lengths before BDC fill:
IF strlen( ls_input-name1 ) > 35.  " KNA1-NAME1 is 35 chars
  ls_input-name1 = ls_input-name1(35).  " Truncate explicitly, log warning
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How would you handle a trap question about BDC and performance tuning?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Performance trap: 'BDC slow hai — kya karte ho?' Wrong answer: 'Nothing, BDC hi slow hota hai.' Sahi answer: performance tune karne ke multiple ways hain. Pehla: MODE 'N' ensure karo — MODE 'A' bahut slow. Doosra: Session Method use karo background mein. Teesra: Parallel background jobs — SM36 se multiple jobs chalaao, each with different record range. Chautha: Package size optimize karo — BDC_OPEN_GROUP mein too many records ek session mein SM35 processing slow karta hai. Paanchva: Consider switching to BAPI — 10x faster typically. Chatha: COMMIT frequency — too frequent commits overhead add karte hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC performance tuning options in order of impact: ensure MODE N is set for all production runs, use Session Method with background processing instead of foreground Call Transaction, split large migrations into parallel background jobs via SM36 with each job handling a different record range, optimize session size at 1000-3000 records per session, and evaluate switching from BDC to BAPI if performance is still insufficient — BAPI is typically 5-10x faster than BDC for equivalent operations. Profile first with a 1000-record test before choosing optimization strategy.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Material master BDC: 100,000 records, estimated 40 hours. Optimizations: MODE N — already set. Split into 10 parallel jobs of 10,000 each. Each job: 4 hours. Total: 4 hours parallel. 10x improvement. Further improvement: switched 3 object types to BAPI — those ran in 30 minutes instead of 4 hours.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Tuning BDC without profiling first — spending hours optimizing the wrong bottleneck. Always measure 100 records with logging to identify where time is spent before tuning.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Parallel BDC via SM36 — split by record range
" Job 1: records 1-10000       Job 2: records 10001-20000
" Each job gets p_from and p_to parameters
SELECT * FROM ztmig_source
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_pkg)
  WHERE seqno BETWEEN @p_from AND @p_to.
" Each job processes its own slice independently
" SM36: schedule 5 jobs simultaneously for 5x throughput

bdc-data-migration-006.easyUnderstanding: Technical design document (TS ya TDD) BDC migration ka blueprint hai — jaise ghar banana se pehle architect ka drawing. Agar drawing nahi hai toh builder apne mann se banata hai — galat ho sakta hai. BDC TS mein yeh cheezein honi chahiye: Source file layout (konsi columns hain, kis format mein), Target transaction (konsa SAP transaction use hoga — XD01, MM01), SHDB recording screens (konsi screens aati hain, konse fields fill karne hain), Field mapping table (source column ABC → SAP field KNA1-NAME1), Error handling approach (failed records ka kya hoga), Cutover plan (kab run karenge, kitna time lagega), Reconciliation query (kaise verify karenge data sahi load hua), Rollback plan (agar fail hua toh kya). Yeh 8 sections ka TS minimum requirement hai professional BDC migration ke liye.
bdc-data-migration-006.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Technical specification document BDC migration ka ek aisa document hai jo developer, functional consultant, aur project manager — teeno ke liye kaam aata hai. Isko systematically explain karo interview mein:

Section 1 — Source File Layout: Source system kya hai (legacy ERP, Excel, flat file, CSV)? File format kya hai (tab-delimited, comma-separated, fixed-width)? Encoding (ASCII, UTF-8)? Column headers aur data types? Sample record dikhao. Mandatory vs optional columns mark karo. Special handling (date formats, decimal separators) document karo.

Section 2 — Target Transaction: SAP transaction code jo migrate karne ke liye use hoga (XD01 for customer, MM01 for material, XK01 for vendor). Transaction mein kitne screens aate hain. Kaunse fields mandatory hain. Kaunse fields BDC se fill honge vs default values. Organization levels (Company code, Plant, Sales Org) jo context provide karte hain.

Section 3 — SHDB Recording Documentation: SHDB se recording ki — screens list karo with dynpro numbers. Har screen pe jo fields fill kiye unki table banao: Screen Number | Field Name (FNAM) | Field Value (FVAL) | Source Column | Notes. Kaunse BDC_OKCODE use kiye (/00 for Enter, =SAVE for Save). Special popups aur unki handling document karo.

Section 4 — Field Mapping Table: Yeh TS ka most important section hai. Table format: Source Field | Source Format | Transformation Rule | SAP Table | SAP Field | SAP Format | Mandatory? | Default Value. Har mapping pe transformation rule document karo — date format convert, leading zero pad, currency strip.

Section 5 — Error Handling: Error table structure (kaunse fields capture honge), error report format (ALV, Excel download), functional team ka process for fixing errors, re-run capability description.

Section 6 — Cutover Plan: Migration sequence (master data pehle, transaction data baad), estimated run time (based on performance test), go/no-go criteria (error percentage threshold), contact list for cutover weekend.

Section 7 — Reconciliation Query: Specific SQL/ABAP query jo post-migration check karta hai. Count check + financial total check if applicable. Who runs it, when, what is acceptable variance.

Section 8 — Rollback Plan: Per-object rollback approach, estimated rollback time, who authorizes rollback decision, go/no-go threshold.
bdc-data-migration-006.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'A BDC technical specification document I prepare includes eight sections: source file layout with column definitions and data types, target SAP transaction with screen flow documentation, SHDB recording with dynpro numbers and field names for each screen, a field mapping table showing source-to-SAP field mapping with transformation rules, error handling design covering error table structure and functional team review process, cutover plan with sequencing and timing, reconciliation query for post-migration validation, and a rollback plan with per-object reversal approach. This document is reviewed and signed off by the functional consultant before development starts — it prevents rework and serves as the test script for UAT.'

Hinglish tip: Field mapping table ko highlight karo — yeh sabse critical part hai. Bolo: 'Mapping table mein sirf source-to-target nahi — transformation rule bhi hoti hai. Jaise source mein date format MM/DD/YYYY hai, SAP mein YYYYMMDD chahiye — yeh rule document karo, tabhi developer aur functional dono aligned hote hain.' Yeh practical depth dikhata hai.
bdc-data-migration-006.codeExamples:
" TS Section 3: SHDB Recording documentation in code
" Screen sequence for XD01 Customer Create:
" Screen 1: SAPMF02D / 0105 — Initial screen
"   BDC fields: RF02D-KUNNR, RF02D-BUKRS, BDC_OKCODE='/00'
" Screen 2: SAPMF02D / 0110 — General Data
"   BDC fields: KNA1-NAME1, KNA1-ORT01, KNA1-LAND1, BDC_OKCODE='/00'
" Screen 3: SAPMF02D / 0120 — Company Code Data
"   BDC fields: KNB1-AKONT, KNB1-ZTERM, BDC_OKCODE='=SAVE'
" Field Mapping Table (document in TS):
" SRC_COL   | SRC_FMT    | TRANSFORMATION    | SAP_FLD      | MANDATORY
" CUST_ID   | 10 chars   | Pad left zero 10  | KNA1-KUNNR   | Y
" FULL_NAME | Free text  | Truncate to 35    | KNA1-NAME1   | Y
" CITY      | Free text  | As-is             | KNA1-ORT01   | N
" COUNTRY   | 2-char ISO | Map to SAP code   | KNA1-LAND1   | Y
" OPEN_DT   | MM/DD/YYYY | Convert YYYYMMDD  | KNB1-ERDAT   | Y

followupAnswerBank.question: Why is field mapping table the most important part of BDC technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Field mapping table wo document hai jahan developer aur functional consultant exactly agree karte hain — source system ka kaunsa field SAP ke kaunse field mein jaayega. Bina mapping table ke developer apne assumption pe kaam karta hai — galat field map ho jaata hai, garbage data SAP mein. Mapping table mein sirf source-to-target nahi — transformation rules bhi hoti hain: date format convert karna, leading zeros add karna, currency code map karna. Yeh document approved hone ke baad development start hota hai — iske baad changes late hote hain aur costly hote hain.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The field mapping table is the contract between developer and functional consultant — it defines exactly which source field maps to which SAP field with what transformation. Without it, developers make assumptions that cause incorrect data in production. The table must include: source field name and data type, transformation rule (date format, leading zero padding, code mapping), target SAP table and field, whether mandatory, and default value if source is blank. This document requires functional sign-off before development starts — post-development mapping changes are expensive and error-prone.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Project lesson: developer mapped source 'Customer Type' code 'C' to SAP account group 'KUNA' — functional team meant 'DEBI'. Mapping table had been verbal, not documented. Wrong account group — customers created with wrong master data. 2000 records had to be corrected. If mapping table had been signed off, this would have been caught in review.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Creating field mapping table only for developer reference without functional review and sign-off — the functional consultant knows the business meaning of each field, the developer knows the SAP target. Both perspectives are needed.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Field mapping table documented as comments in code
" Source: LEGACY_CUSTOMER table | Target: KNA1
" SRC: CUST_CODE (VARCHAR 10) → KNA1-KUNNR (CHAR 10)
"   Rule: Pad with leading zeros to 10 chars
"   lv_kunnr = |{ ls_src-cust_code ALPHA = IN WIDTH = 10 }|
" SRC: CUST_TYPE (CHAR 1: C/I/G) → KNA1-KTOKD (CHAR 4)
"   Rule: C→DEBI, I→KUNA, G→KORG  (agreed with functional)
"   lv_ktokd = SWITCH #( ls_src-cust_type
"              WHEN 'C' THEN 'DEBI' WHEN 'I' THEN 'KUNA'
"              WHEN 'G' THEN 'KORG' ELSE 'DEBI' ).

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document the cutover plan in a BDC migration technical spec?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Cutover plan document mein specific timing honi chahiye — vague 'Friday night' nahi. Acha cutover plan: Date aur time (Friday Nov 29, 10 PM), System freeze time (last transaction kab hogi), Migration sequence with timings (Step 1: Vendor master — 10 PM to 2 AM, 3 hours + 1 hour buffer), Go/no-go checkpoint (Saturday 3 AM — if >5% error, rollback decision by project manager), Validation window (Saturday 6 AM to 9 AM — functional team validates), Go-live time (Saturday 10 AM). Contact list (project manager, SAP basis, developer, functional lead) with mobile numbers. Escalation path.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The cutover plan section of the TS includes: freeze date and time for the source system (no new changes after this point), migration sequence with estimated start and end times for each object type, go/no-go decision checkpoints with error rate thresholds and decision authority, validation window timeline for functional team sign-off, planned go-live time, contact list with escalation path, and rollback trigger criteria. Timings come from mock cutover runs — never estimate without running at least one dress rehearsal.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Our TS cutover plan had timestamps per job: Vendor master 10PM-1AM (3h), Customer master 1AM-4AM (3h), Material master 4AM-7AM (3h), Open items 7AM-10AM (3h), Validation 10AM-12PM (2h), Go-live 12PM. Total: 14 hours window. Mock cutover confirmed 12 hours actual. 2 hour buffer used for an unexpected open item reconciliation issue.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Writing cutover plan without timing from actual mock run — estimated timings from documentation are always wrong. Always derive cutover timings from measured mock cutover runs.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Cutover sequence documentation (code comment format)
" CUTOVER PLAN — Project: Vendor Migration — Date: 2024-11-29
" Step 1: 22:00 — ZMIG_VENDOR_MASTER (5000 records, est 2h)
"   Error threshold: >3% → escalate. >10% → rollback decision.
" Step 2: 00:00 — ZMIG_VENDOR_BANK (5000 records, est 1h)
"   Prerequisite: Step 1 complete with <3% error
" Step 3: 01:00 — ZMIG_OPEN_ITEMS (20000 records, est 3h)
" Checkpoint: 04:00 — Go/No-Go review by PM + Functional Lead
" Step 4: 09:00 — Validation run by functional team
" Go-live: 11:00 or after functional sign-off

followupAnswerBank.question: What is a reconciliation query and how do you document it in the TS?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Reconciliation query wo specific SQL ya ABAP code hai jo migration ke baad check karta hai ki sab sahi load hua ya nahi. Documentation mein yeh include karo: query purpose (count check ya financial total), exact ABAP/SQL code (copy-paste ready), expected output, acceptable variance (count: 0 variance, financial: 0 variance), who runs it, when (immediately after migration, again after validation). Ek simple count query aur ek financial total query — dono document karo in TS.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The reconciliation query section documents specific, executable ABAP SELECT statements or reports that validate migration completeness. I document: count reconciliation query comparing source file record count against SAP records created, financial total reconciliation for monetary objects comparing sum from source against SAP, spot-check query listing 20 random migrated records for manual field verification, and any object-specific business validations. The section specifies who runs each query, at what point in the cutover, and what constitutes pass/fail.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Vendor open items TS reconciliation section: Query 1: COUNT of BSIK records per vendor where posting date in migration range. Query 2: SUM(DMBTR) per vendor. Expected: count matches source file count exactly, amounts match to last decimal. Variance = escalation to project manager. This section was directly used by the functional team during cutover validation — they ran it themselves without developer involvement.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Documenting reconciliation as 'verify in SE16N' without specifying exact query — different people interpret this differently, leading to inconsistent validation. Always provide the exact executable query.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Reconciliation queries documented in TS
" Query 1: Count check
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM kna1
  WHERE kunnr IN ( SELECT kunnr FROM ztmig_loaded
                   WHERE run_id = 'PROD_2024_01' )
  INTO @DATA(lv_sap_cnt).
" Compare with: source file count = 5000
" Pass criterion: lv_sap_cnt = 5000
" Query 2: Financial total (for FI objects)
SELECT SUM( dmbtr ) FROM bsik
  WHERE lifnr IN @lt_migrated_vendors
  INTO @DATA(lv_total).
" Compare with: source system total = 1234567.89
" Pass criterion: ABS( lv_total - 1234567.89 ) = 0

followupAnswerBank.question: How should you document error handling design in a BDC technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Error handling design document mein answer karo: (1) Error kab capture hoga — CALL TRANSACTION ke baad sy-subrc check, (2) Error table structure — kaunse fields (key field, error message, timestamp), (3) Error report format — ALV mein dikhega ya Excel download, (4) Error report audience — functional team kyunki woh data fix karenge, (5) Correction process — functional team Excel mein fix kare, developer ko wapas de, re-run karo sirf failed records, (6) Re-run safety — duplicate nahi banenge, (7) Final disposition — maximum kitne re-runs, phir kya (manual entry).
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Error handling design documentation covers: the exact sy-subrc check after each CALL TRANSACTION, the error log table structure with key fields (record key, original input values, SAP message type and text, timestamp, username), the format of the error report presented to the functional team, the correction workflow (functional team fixes source data, provides corrected file, developer re-runs for failed records only), re-run safety mechanism to prevent duplicates, and escalation path for records that fail repeatedly after multiple correction attempts.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Error handling section of our TS included a process flow: Failed record → Error log table ZTMIG_ERRORS → ALV report → Email to functional lead → Functional team corrects Excel → Developer loads corrected file → Re-run with mode 'RERUN' that skips already-processed records. This process was followed exactly during cutover — functional team knew exactly what to do when they got the error report.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Documenting error handling only as 'errors will be logged' without specifying the log structure, report format, correction process, and re-run capability — this leaves functional team without a clear action path when they receive a list of failed records.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Error handling: TS-defined structure
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_error_log,
  run_id   TYPE string,          " Migration run identifier
  rec_key  TYPE string,          " Primary key (e.g. customer number)
  input_01 TYPE string,          " Original field 1 value
  input_02 TYPE string,          " Original field 2 value
  msg_type TYPE c LENGTH 1,      " E/W/I from SAP
  msg_text TYPE string,          " SAP error message text
  uname    TYPE sy-uname,
  ertime   TYPE timestampl,
END OF ty_error_log.
" This structure approved by functional team in TS review

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the rollback plan section in a migration TS and what must it contain?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Rollback plan TS mein hona zaroori hai kyunki: agar migration fail ho gayi toh project manager ko real-time decision karna padta hai — rollback karo ya continue karo. Bina documented plan ke yeh decision mein ghante lag jaate hain. TS mein: har object ke liye reversal approach (customer delete via XD06, FI document reverse via FB08), estimated rollback time (3 hours vendor reversal), go/no-go error threshold (>5% error = rollback trigger), who decides rollback (project manager + client sign-off), rollback execution steps (who does what), communication plan (stakeholders ko kab batate hain).
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The rollback plan section must contain: per-object reversal approach with the specific SAP transaction or custom program used for reversal, estimated time to complete rollback for each object type, the error rate threshold that triggers rollback decision, the decision authority (typically project manager with client sign-off), execution steps and responsible persons, and a communication plan for informing stakeholders if rollback is triggered. Critical: some objects like FI posted documents cannot be easily reversed — for these, the strategy is delayed commit (validate before finalizing) or accepting manual reversal cost with management sign-off.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Our TS rollback plan: vendor master — ZMIG_ROLLBACK_VENDOR program (custom mass deletion, 30 min for 5000 records), customer master — standard mass deletion via ZSAPDELCUST, open items — session not committed until validation complete (30-second rollback). Trigger: PM + client decides at go/no-go checkpoint if >5% error. This plan was rehearsed in mock cutover.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Writing 'rollback by deleting records' without having a tested deletion program ready — manual deletion of 5000 records takes days, not hours. Build and test the rollback program as part of migration development.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Rollback program skeleton (developed alongside migration)
REPORT zmig_rollback_vendor.
SELECT lifnr FROM ztmig_loaded
  WHERE run_id = p_runid AND status = 'S'
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_to_delete).
" Delete only records this run created
LOOP AT lt_to_delete ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<v>).
  CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_VENDOR_DELETE'  " Or custom deletion
    EXPORTING lifnr = <v>-lifnr.
ENDLOOP.
CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT' EXPORTING wait = 'X'.
WRITE: / |Rollback complete: { lines( lt_to_delete ) } records reversed|.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does the TS documentation differ between BDC and BAPI migration approaches?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: BDC TS aur BAPI TS mein kuch key differences hain. BDC TS additional sections chahti hai: SHDB recording detail (dynpro numbers, screen sequence), BDC field name mapping (FNAM values exact), MODE strategy (A/E/N), screen-specific notes (popup handling). BAPI TS simpler hai: BAPI function module name, import parameters (ls_header, ls_address), table parameters (lt_return), commit strategy (batch commit every 200 records). BAPI TS mein SHDB section nahi hoti — no screen dependency. Common sections dono ke liye: source file layout, field mapping, error handling, cutover plan, reconciliation, rollback.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC technical spec includes SHDB recording documentation with dynpro numbers and screen sequence, BDC FNAM field name table, MODE parameter strategy, and popup handling notes — all specific to screen simulation. BAPI technical spec replaces these with: the specific BAPI function module name with its parameter structure (import/export/tables), commit strategy (batch size for BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT), and BAPI return code handling logic. Both share common sections: source file layout, field mapping table, error handling, cutover plan, reconciliation, and rollback. The BAPI spec is typically shorter and more maintainable — no screen-specific documentation to update after SAP patches.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: We maintained two types of TS templates in our team: BDC_TS_TEMPLATE.docx and BAPI_TS_TEMPLATE.docx. Developer chose correct template at project start. Client review was faster for BAPI template — no screen documentation to review. BDC template review took 2 hours, BAPI template 45 minutes. Functional team found BAPI mapping table easier to understand.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Using the same TS template for both BDC and BAPI without including or excluding relevant sections — BAPI TS with SHDB documentation is confusing, BDC TS without screen sequence is incomplete.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" BAPI TS documentation format (in code comments)
" BAPI: BAPI_CUSTOMER_CREATEFROMDATA1
" IMPORT params:
"   pi_kunnr_range: customer number range object
" TABLES params:
"   pi_addressdata: address fields (name, street, city, country)
"   pi_centraldata: central data (account group, customer class)
"   return: result messages (type E = error)
" Commit: BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT every 500 records
" Error check: IF line_exists( return[ type = 'E' ] )
" No dynpro documentation needed — BAPI is screen-independent

followupAnswerBank.question: Who should review and sign off on a BDC migration technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: TS review multi-stakeholder process hai. Developer khud writes karta hai — but review sirf developer se nahi hona chahiye. Sign-off chain: Functional Consultant: field mapping correct hai, business logic sahi hai, error handling approach functional team ke liye usable hai. Technical Lead ya Senior Developer: code approach correct hai, performance strategy sahi hai, error handling robust hai. Basis Team: cutover window approved hai, background job scheduling possible hai, authorization setup feasible hai. Client (optional but recommended): cutover plan approved, go/no-go criteria agreed. Missing sign-off = project risk.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: BDC migration TS should have sign-off from: functional consultant confirming field mapping business accuracy and error handling usability, technical lead validating code approach and performance strategy, Basis team approving cutover window and confirming authorization setup feasibility, and ideally client project manager acknowledging the cutover plan and go/no-go thresholds. Each sign-off has a specific scope — functional validates business logic, technical validates technical approach, Basis validates infrastructure, client validates timeline. Missing any one creates a gap that surfaces as a production issue.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Lesson from project: TS had functional and technical sign-off but not Basis. Cutover day: Basis team said background jobs cannot run on Sunday due to maintenance window. Cutover had to be rescheduled by 2 weeks. If Basis had reviewed TS, they would have flagged this conflict in week 2 of the project, not week 8.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Getting only developer and functional sign-off on TS while skipping Basis and client — infrastructure constraints and business calendar conflicts discovered in TS review are far cheaper to resolve than cutover weekend surprises.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" TS document header (approval tracking)
" Document: BDC Technical Specification — Customer Master Migration
" Version: 1.2  Status: Approved
" Prepared by: Developer Name  Date: 2024-10-01
" Reviewed by:
"   Functional Lead: Jane Doe  Sign-off: 2024-10-05
"   Technical Lead:  John Smith Sign-off: 2024-10-06
"   Basis Lead:      Bob Kumar  Sign-off: 2024-10-07
"   Project Manager: Sara Lee   Sign-off: 2024-10-08
" Change log: v1.1 field mapping updated, v1.2 cutover window revised

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you document the source file layout in a BDC technical specification?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Source file layout documentation detailed honi chahiye — functional team jo file provide karegi, woh exactly isi format mein honi chahiye. Document karo: file format (CSV, tab-delimited, fixed-width, Excel), encoding (UTF-8, ASCII), header row hai ya nahi, delimiter (comma, pipe, tab), column list with sequence number, column header name (exactly as in file), data type (text, number, date), max length, mandatory/optional, format rules (date: MM/DD/YYYY, amount: no currency symbol, no comma separator), sample values. Ek sample row bhi document karo. Yeh file format document functional team ko send karo — unhe exact format mein data prepare karna hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Source file layout documentation includes: file format and encoding, delimiter specification, header row presence, a numbered column table with column name, data type, maximum field length, mandatory/optional flag, format rules for dates and numbers, valid value list for code fields, and a sample row. This document is provided to the functional team as the data preparation template — they fill data following exactly this specification. Deviations from the specification are caught in the pre-migration validation program and reported before any BDC run.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Source file specification sent to functional team. First file received: dates in MM-DD-YYYY (hyphens not slashes), amounts with currency symbols (USD 1,234.56), some mandatory columns blank. Validation program caught all 3 issues before BDC ran. Functional team corrected and resubmitted. Second file: 0 validation errors. BDC ran cleanly. The specification document prevented bad data from reaching BDC.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Sending a vague 'please prepare the data in Excel' instruction to functional team without a detailed column specification — results in inconsistent data formats that the validation program and BDC program must handle ad-hoc.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Source file specification validation
" Validate date column format BEFORE BDC run
CONSTANTS: c_date_pattern TYPE string VALUE '##/##/####'.
LOOP AT lt_source ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  " Check date format MM/DD/YYYY
  IF NOT <r>-open_date CO '0123456789/'.
    APPEND VALUE #( col = 'OPEN_DATE'
                    val = <r>-open_date
                    err = 'Invalid date format — use MM/DD/YYYY' ) TO lt_val_errors.
  ENDIF.
  " Check mandatory field
  IF <r>-cust_code IS INITIAL.
    APPEND VALUE #( col = 'CUST_CODE' err = 'Mandatory field blank' ) TO lt_val_errors.
  ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

bdc-data-migration-007.easyUnderstanding: Effort estimation matlab pehle estimate karo kitna kaam hai, phir project mein accordingly plan karo. BDC estimation ke simple benchmarks jo real projects se aaye hain: Simple BDC (5-10 screens, ek transaction) — 3 se 5 din. Complex BDC (multiple transactions, error handling, parallel processing) — 2 se 3 hafte. LSMW (configuration-based, no coding) — 1 se 2 din per object. Ek pura module migration (jaise sab vendors) — 4 se 6 hafte including testing. Yeh benchmarks tab ke liye hain jab developer experienced hai aur requirements clear hain. Agar requirements unclear hain toh 20-30% buffer add karo. Agar naya developer hai toh 50% add karo. Estimation mein sirf coding time mat socho — design, unit test, UAT support, error fix, re-run, documentation bhi hote hain. Real estimation formula: (development + unit test) * 1.5 for buffer + UAT support time + documentation time.
bdc-data-migration-007.hinglishMasterExplanation:
Effort estimation ek skill hai jo junior aur senior developer ko alag karta hai. Interview mein 'how do you estimate BDC effort' poochha jaaye toh structured answer do:

Pehla step — Requirements analysis: Estimate start karne se pehle requirements clear karo. Kitne source fields hain? Kitni transactions hain? Kya multiple screens chahiye? Error handling kitna complex hoga? Volume kitna hai? Yeh questions answer kiye bina estimate impossible hai.

Doosra step — Complexity classification:
- Simple BDC: Single transaction, 5-10 screens, straightforward field mapping, 500-5000 records. Estimate: 3-5 days (design 0.5 day, SHDB + code 2 days, unit test 1 day, documentation 0.5 day).
- Medium BDC: Multiple transactions ya screens (10-20), complex field transformations (date formats, code mapping, lookups), error handling with ALV report, 5000-50000 records. Estimate: 8-12 days.
- Complex BDC: Multiple dependent transactions (create vendor, then bank details, then contact person — sequence dependent), large volume (100K+) requiring parallel processing, integration with other modules, custom rollback program. Estimate: 15-20 days.
- LSMW: Standard object, configuration-based, no ABAP. Estimate: 1-2 days per object.
- Full module migration: All objects in one SAP module (all vendors including master + bank + open items). Estimate: 4-6 weeks including 2 weeks for testing.

Teesra step — Non-coding effort: Developers underestimate non-coding work. Add karo: Requirements clarification (10-15% of dev estimate), Unit testing (30-40% of dev estimate), UAT support — functional team queries answer karna, error investigation (20-30% of dev estimate), Documentation (10-15%), Knowledge transfer (5-10%), Buffer for scope creep (20%).

Chauta step — Volume-based adjustment: 100K+ records mein parallel processing design karna padta hai — add 2-3 days for architecture. Network/system latency for large volume test runs — add 1-2 days for performance testing and optimization.

Paanchva step — Risk factors: Unclear mapping — add 20%. New developer — add 50%. Legacy system without documentation — add 30%. Complex validations — add 25%.
bdc-data-migration-007.interviewMeKyaBolnaHai:
Interview Me Bolna Hai:
'I estimate BDC and data migration effort using complexity classification. For a simple BDC — single transaction, 5-10 screens, standard field mapping — I estimate 3 to 5 days covering design, SHDB recording, development, unit test with error cases, and documentation. For complex multi-transaction BDC with large volume requiring parallel processing, the estimate is 2 to 3 weeks. LSMW configuration for a standard object is 1 to 2 days. A full module migration covering all objects with testing is 4 to 6 weeks. I always add 20% buffer for scope clarifications and apply a multiplier for unclear requirements. Critically, my estimate includes not just coding time but also UAT support, error investigation, re-run support, and documentation — non-coding work is typically 40 to 50% of total effort in my experience.'

Hinglish tip: Interviewer impression ke liye yeh add karo: 'Mere estimate mein non-coding time bhi hota hai — requirements clarification, UAT support, error investigation. Yeh 40-50% hota hai total effort ka. Jo developers sirf coding time estimate karte hain unke projects always delay hote hain.' Yeh mature, experience-backed statement hai jo interviewer ko impress karta hai.
bdc-data-migration-007.codeExamples:
" Effort estimation support: automated complexity indicator
REPORT zest_bdc_complexity.
PARAMETERS: p_screens TYPE i DEFAULT 5,
             p_trans   TYPE i DEFAULT 1,
             p_volume  TYPE i DEFAULT 1000,
             p_errors  TYPE c AS CHECKBOX DEFAULT 'X'.
DATA: lv_days TYPE p DECIMALS 1.
" Base estimate by screen count
lv_days = SWITCH #( p_screens
  WHEN 1 THEN 1  WHEN 2 THEN 2  ELSE p_screens / 3 ).
" Adjust for multiple transactions
lv_days = lv_days + ( p_trans - 1 ) * 2.
" Adjust for volume (>10K needs performance work)
IF p_volume > 10000. lv_days = lv_days + 2. ENDIF.
" Error handling + testing overhead
IF p_errors = 'X'.  lv_days = lv_days * 1.4. ENDIF.
" 20% buffer
lv_days = lv_days * 1.2.
WRITE: / |Estimated effort: { lv_days } days (includes test + doc)|.

followupAnswerBank.question: What are the key inputs you need before estimating BDC migration effort?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Estimate karne se pehle yeh information chahiye: (1) Source system — legacy ERP ya Excel? File format? Column count? (2) Target transaction — XD01 simple hai, multi-screen complex hai. (3) Volume — 500 records easy, 500,000 records architecture problem. (4) Field mapping complexity — straight copy easy, date format conversion, code mapping, lookups hard. (5) Error handling requirements — simple log ya full ALV report with re-run? (6) Timeline — deadline kab hai, cutover window kitna bada? (7) Team experience — naya developer ya experienced? Bina yeh inputs ke estimate sirf guess hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Before estimating I need: source system type and file format, target SAP transaction with number of screens, record volume, field mapping complexity including any transformations or code lookups, error handling requirements, whether a rollback program is needed, cutover timeline constraints, and team experience level. Without these inputs any estimate is a guess. I ask for a sample source file and a 10-record test dataset before finalizing estimates — these reveal hidden complexities like special characters, code mismatches, and unexpected mandatory fields.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimation meeting: client said 'simple customer migration, about 1000 records.' I asked for sample file.
