Sample file had 80 columns, 15 different date formats, 6 legacy system codes that mapped to SAP in non-obvious ways, and records in 3 languages. Revised estimate: 8 days (not the 3 I would have said without the sample file).
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Providing estimates in the first meeting before seeing actual data — the phrase 'simple migration' from a client almost always hides complexity that only the data reveals.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Complexity assessment from sample data
LOOP AT lt_sample ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  " Count distinct date formats
  IF <r>-date_col CA '-'. lv_dash_dates = lv_dash_dates + 1. ENDIF.
  IF <r>-date_col CA '/'. lv_slash_dates = lv_slash_dates + 1. ENDIF.
  " Count distinct code values needing mapping
  COLLECT VALUE #( code = <r>-type_code ) INTO lt_distinct_codes.
ENDLOOP.
WRITE: / |Date format variants: { lv_dash_dates } dash, { lv_slash_dates } slash|.
WRITE: / |Distinct type codes to map: { lines( lt_distinct_codes ) }|.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate testing effort for BDC migration?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Testing effort ko underestimate mat karo — yeh bahut common mistake hai. Testing mein kya aata hai: Unit testing (developer khud karta hai): 30-40% of development time. UAT support (functional team test kar rahi hai, developer available hai questions ke liye): 20-25% of development time. Bug fixing after UAT: 15-20% of development time. Performance testing (large volume): additional 1-2 days. Mock cutover support: 1 day per mock run. Re-run testing: 0.5 day. Total testing effort typically equal karta hai development effort ko — agar development 5 days hai toh testing 4-5 days. Total project effort: development + testing = 2x development estimate.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Testing effort for BDC migration: unit testing is 30-40% of development time and is done by the developer — testing positive cases, error cases, re-run safety, and MODE N behavior. UAT support where the functional team validates is 20-25% of development — developer is available for questions, error investigation, and quick fixes. Bug fixing after UAT rounds adds 15-20%. For large volume migrations add a dedicated performance testing phase of 1-2 days. Mock cutover support is 1 day per mock run with minimum 2 mocks recommended. Total testing effort typically equals total development effort — a 5-day development is a 10-day total project including all testing phases.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimation retrospective: development took 4 days as estimated. Testing breakdown: unit test 1.5 days, UAT support 3 days (functional team found 2 mapping bugs + 1 error handling issue), bug fix 1 day, mock cutover support 2 days. Total: 7.5 days testing for 4 days development. Ratio: 1.9x. Now I always estimate total project as 2x development estimate.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Estimating only development time and adding '20% for testing' — testing in data migration is closer to 80-100% of development effort, not 20%. Underestimating testing causes project delays.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Effort breakdown template (used in project planning)
" BDC Migration: Customer Master (Simple — 8 screens, 5000 records)
" Development phases:
"   Requirements + SHDB analysis:  0.5 days
"   BDC program development:       2.0 days
"   Error handling + log:          0.5 days
"   Documentation:                 0.5 days
"   Subtotal development:          3.5 days
" Testing phases:
"   Unit test (positive + error):  1.5 days
"   UAT support:                   1.5 days
"   Bug fix post-UAT:              0.5 days
"   Mock cutover support:          1.0 day
"   Subtotal testing:              4.5 days
" TOTAL ESTIMATE:                  8.0 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How does migration volume affect effort estimation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Volume sirf run time nahi affect karta — design bhi affect karta hai. Kam volume (<5000 records): simple sequential processing, no parallel jobs needed. Medium volume (5K-50K): package processing banana padega, performance test karna padega. Large volume (50K-500K): parallel job architecture design karna padega, restart/checkpoint mechanism banana padega, performance optimization karna padega — yeh 2-3 extra days hai. Very large volume (500K+): full parallel processing design, database performance considerations, may need Basis team involvement for system sizing. Volume increase se yeh additional effort aata hai — capture karo estimate mein.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Volume affects both runtime and architectural complexity. Below 5000 records: standard sequential BDC or BAPI suffices, no special architecture. 5000 to 50000: add package-based processing and a performance test phase — 1 additional day. 50000 to 500000: parallel background job design, checkpoint restart mechanism, performance tuning — 3 to 5 additional days. Above 500000: full parallel architecture, possibly database partitioning considerations, Basis team coordination for system resource allocation — 5 to 7 additional days. Document the volume assumption in the estimate — if volume changes significantly, the estimate must be revised.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Initial estimate for material master: 8000 records — 5 days. Halfway through project client revealed actual volume: 85,000 records. Parallel processing architecture needed: 3 additional days design. Performance test: 1 additional day. Restart mechanism: 1 day. Total addition: 5 days — estimate became 10 days. Volume assumption change = estimate change. Always document volume assumption explicitly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Not documenting the volume assumption in the estimate — when volume doubles, developers absorb the extra work without scope change discussion, causing untracked overrun.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Volume-based architecture decision
CONSTANTS: c_simple_limit   TYPE i VALUE 5000,
           c_parallel_limit TYPE i VALUE 50000.
IF lv_total_records <= c_simple_limit.
  " Simple sequential: no special architecture
  PERFORM run_sequential USING lt_all_records.
ELSEIF lv_total_records <= c_parallel_limit.
  " Package processing: loop with package size
  PERFORM run_packaged USING lt_all_records c_pkg_size.
ELSE.
  " Parallel jobs: SM36 scheduling + restart logic needed
  " This path requires 3-5 extra dev days — flag in estimate
  PERFORM run_parallel USING p_from p_to.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between development effort and project effort in migration estimation?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Development effort aur project effort alag hain — confusion se project plans galat bante hain. Development effort: sirf ABAP coding ka time — SHDB karna, program likhna, unit test karna. Project effort: saari activities ka total — requirements, design, development, unit test, UAT support, bug fix, documentation, mock cutover, actual cutover support, knowledge transfer. Real projects mein project effort development effort ka 2.5 se 3x hota hai. Agar developer bolta hai '5 din mein code ho jaayega' — project manager plan karo 12-15 din including all activities.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Development effort covers only coding activities: SHDB recording, program development, and developer-run unit testing. Project effort includes all activities from requirements gathering to go-live: requirements clarification (10-15%), technical specification writing (10%), development (35-40%), unit testing including error cases (15%), UAT support and bug fixing (20-25%), documentation (5-10%), and cutover support including mock runs (10-15%). Project effort is typically 2.5 to 3x development effort. Estimating only development time and presenting it as project timeline causes consistent project overruns.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Estimation to stakeholders: 'BDC will take 5 days to develop.' Stakeholder plans 5-day timeline. Actual project: requirements 2 days, development 5 days, testing 4 days, UAT 3 days, bug fix 2 days, documentation 1 day, mock cutover 2 days. Total: 19 days. Stakeholder upset. Now I always present project effort not just development effort.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Presenting development-only estimates as project timelines to stakeholders — leads to unrealistic expectations and project delivery conflicts. Always break down full project effort when presenting to project managers.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Estimation breakdown for stakeholder presentation
" Phase 1 — Analysis & Design
"   Source file analysis + requirements:  2 days
"   Technical specification writing:       1 day
"   TS review + sign-off:                 1 day  (depends on availability)
" Phase 2 — Development
"   SHDB recording + BDC coding:          3 days
"   Error handling + validation:          1 day
"   Unit testing (all cases):             1.5 days
" Phase 3 — Testing & Validation
"   UAT support + bug fixing:             3 days
"   Performance testing (if >10K recs):   1 day
" Phase 4 — Cutover
"   Mock cutover x2 runs:                 2 days
"   Actual cutover support:               1 day
"   Documentation + KT:                  1 day
" TOTAL PROJECT EFFORT: ~17.5 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort for LSMW versus custom BDC development?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: LSMW estimation simple hai — standard objects ke liye 1-2 days per object. LSMW mein koi ABAP coding nahi — configuration-based hai. Steps: LSMW transaction kholo, object select karo, field mapping configure karo, test karo, run karo. Time breakdown: 1 hour setup, 2-3 hours field mapping, 2-4 hours testing = 1 day total typically. Complex LSMW (custom subobjects, complex mapping): 2 days. Custom BDC vs LSMW: BDC zyada time lagta hai (3-5 days vs 1-2 days) but zyada flexible hai. Use karo LSMW jab standard object hai — save 2-3 days per object. Use karo BDC jab LSMW object available nahi, custom transaction hai, ya complex logic chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: LSMW estimation is typically 1 to 2 days per standard migration object — 0.5 day for setup and object selection, 0.5 to 1 day for field mapping configuration and testing, 0.5 day for production run and validation. No ABAP coding means no compilation, no ABAP unit test. Compare this to custom BDC for the same object: 3 to 5 days. For projects with 10 standard objects, LSMW saves 20 to 30 development days versus custom BDC. The tradeoff: LSMW is less flexible and limited to SAP-delivered objects. Estimation decision: enumerate all migration objects, classify each as LSMW-suitable or requiring custom BDC, sum respective estimates.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Migration project with 20 objects: 12 standard (customer, vendor, material, GL accounts, cost centers) — LSMW suitable: 12 * 1.5 days = 18 days. 8 custom Z-transactions — BDC required: 8 * 4 days = 32 days. Total: 50 days. All custom BDC: 20 * 4 = 80 days. LSMW saved 30 days = significant project budget saving. This analysis should be done at project planning stage.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Defaulting to custom BDC for all objects without checking if LSMW can handle standard objects — losing 2-3 days per object that could have been done in LSMW.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" LSMW vs BDC decision per migration object
" Object 1: Customer master — LSMW standard object exists
"   Method: LSMW (RMDATIND customer)  Estimate: 1.5 days
" Object 2: Material master — LSMW standard object exists
"   Method: LSMW (RMDATIND material)  Estimate: 1.5 days
" Object 3: Custom price table ZTPRICE — no LSMW object
"   Method: Custom BDC for ZPRC01     Estimate: 4 days
" Object 4: Vendor master — LSMW standard object exists
"   Method: LSMW                       Estimate: 1 day
" Total: 8 days (vs 16 days all BDC) — LSMW saves 8 days

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you communicate estimate changes when scope increases during a BDC migration project?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Scope change communication ek important skill hai — technical nahi, project management skill hai. Jab scope badhe: immediately identify karo impact. Document karo: original scope kya tha, kya badla, new estimate kya hai, iska impact timeline pe kya hai. Project manager ko written communication do — email ya meeting notes. Bolo: 'Original estimate 8 days tha based on 5000 records and single transaction. Volume 50,000 ho gayi, 3 transactions add ho gayi — revised estimate 18 days. Timeline impact: 10 days delay unless resources add karo ya scope cut karo.' Bina clear communication ke developer absorbs scope silently — burnout aur project overrun.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When scope increases during a BDC migration project, I immediately document the change: original scope assumption, what changed, revised effort breakdown, and timeline impact. I communicate this in writing to the project manager before absorbing additional work — a change request or scope change note. The message clearly states: original estimate was based on specific assumptions, those assumptions changed, here is the revised effort and timeline impact, and here are options to manage the change (add resources, reduce scope, extend timeline). Silent scope absorption is a risk — it causes overrun without visibility.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Midway through project: client added 3 new migration objects. Developer said 'I'll manage.' 2 weeks later: project was 10 days late, developer was stressed, client was upset. Correct action: immediately escalate to PM — 'Adding 3 objects adds 12 days. Options: extend by 12 days, add another developer, or defer 3 objects to Phase 2.' Let PM and client decide — not developer.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Agreeing to additional scope without formally documenting the estimate impact — being helpful in the moment causes project failure later. Always escalate scope changes formally.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Scope change impact template (email format)
" Subject: Scope Change Alert — BDC Migration Project
" Original scope: Customer master only (5000 records, 1 transaction)
" Original estimate: 8 project days
" Change requested: Add vendor master + open items (15000 records, 2 transactions)
" Additional effort breakdown:
"   Vendor master BDC: 4 days
"   Open items BAPI:   3 days
"   Additional testing: 3 days
"   Additional mock cutover: 1 day
"   Total addition: 11 days
" Revised estimate: 19 project days
" Timeline impact: +11 days unless additional resource assigned
" Decision required by: [date] to maintain go-live

followupAnswerBank.question: What buffers do you add to BDC estimation and why?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Buffer estimation ek art hai — zyada buffer do toh project manager khush nahi, kam do toh tum struggle karte ho. Standard buffers jo real projects mein use hote hain: Requirements ambiguity: 20% if requirements somewhat unclear, 30% if very unclear. Naya developer: 40-50% additional on development estimate. New-to-developer transaction: 20% extra for learning curve. Multiple stakeholders for sign-off: 10-15% for review cycles. Data quality unknown: 25% for discovery and handling unexpected formats. S/4HANA compatibility checks: 10% if upgrading to S/4HANA alongside migration. Total buffer should not exceed 50% — agar zyada uncertainty hai toh time-and-material engagement recommend karo fixed price nahi.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Buffers I apply in BDC estimation: 20% for standard scope uncertainty (requirements that seem clear often have hidden edge cases), 30% for unclear requirements needing multiple clarification rounds, 40% when the developer is new to the specific SAP module, 25% when source data quality is unknown and a discovery phase is needed, 15% for stakeholder review and sign-off cycles, 10% for regression testing after transport in multi-system landscapes. The maximum total buffer I apply is 50% — beyond that I recommend a time-and-material engagement with phased billing rather than a fixed-price commitment, because fixed estimates with >50% uncertainty are project risks not estimates.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Fixed price project: estimated 15 days with 20% buffer = 18 days. Actual: 26 days. Lost 8 days of revenue. Root cause: source data quality was completely unknown — first time seeing legacy data revealed 30 distinct date formats, 500 obsolete code values, duplicate records. Should have recommended T&M with discovery phase or applied 40% uncertainty buffer for unknown data quality.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Applying a flat 10-20% buffer to all estimates regardless of uncertainty level — low-uncertainty projects don't need it, high-uncertainty projects need 30-50% or a T&M engagement recommendation.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Buffer calculation helper
DATA: lv_base_days    TYPE p DECIMALS 1 VALUE '8.0',
      lv_buffer_pct   TYPE p DECIMALS 2,
      lv_final_days   TYPE p DECIMALS 1.
" Requirements clarity: clear=1.20, partial=1.30, unclear=1.50
lv_buffer_pct = 1.20.  " Clear requirements
" Data quality unknown: add 25%
lv_buffer_pct = lv_buffer_pct + 0.25.
" New developer: add 40%
" lv_buffer_pct = lv_buffer_pct + 0.40.  " Uncomment if applicable
lv_final_days = lv_base_days * lv_buffer_pct.
WRITE: / |Base estimate : { lv_base_days } days|.
WRITE: / |Buffer factor: { lv_buffer_pct }|.
WRITE: / |Final estimate: { lv_final_days } days|.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you estimate effort when the client says 'just a simple migration' but you suspect complexity?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Yeh bahut common scenario hai — client always kehta hai 'simple hai, jaldi ho jaayega.' Experienced developer ka approach: 'Sure, let me quickly look at the source data to confirm.' Sample file maango — 20-50 records ka. Analysis karo: kitne columns hain, date formats kaun kaun se, codes kya hain, mandatory fields kaun se, volume estimate source se nikalo, transactions check karo SHDB mein. 30 minutes mein complexity visible ho jaati hai. Phir honest estimate do — explain karo kyun yeh 'simple' se zyada hai. Client ko educate karo — log appreciate karte hain honest assessment jab backed by evidence ho.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: When a client describes a migration as 'simple,' I request a sample file before confirming the estimate. A 20-50 record sample reveals: actual column count versus described, date format variety, code values requiring mapping tables, data quality issues, and unexpected mandatory fields. I analyze the sample and base the estimate on what the data actually shows, not what the client described. If complexity is found, I explain it with the data as evidence — 'Your date column has 5 different formats which requires a robust conversion routine — that adds 1.5 days.' Evidence-based estimates are accepted better than gut-feel revisions.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Client: 'Simple vendor migration, maybe 2 days?' I asked for sample file. Sample: 60 columns (expected 20), 4 bank account types in legacy needing different BDC screens, date formats in German locale, vendors in multiple company codes. Revised estimate: 8 days. Client surprised but accepted when I showed sample file analysis. If I had said '2 days' without looking, we would have had a problem by day 3.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Accepting 'simple' at face value and estimating without data — the word 'simple' is the client's perception, not technical reality. Always verify with actual data before committing to an estimate.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Quick complexity assessment from sample file (30 min analysis)
DATA: lv_col_count   TYPE i,
      lt_date_fmts   TYPE string_table,
      lt_code_values TYPE string_table.
" Count columns in header row
SPLIT lt_raw[ 1 ]-data AT ',' INTO TABLE DATA(lt_cols).
lv_col_count = lines( lt_cols ).
WRITE: / |Columns found: { lv_col_count }| COLOR COL_TOTAL.
" Check date format variety
LOOP AT lt_raw FROM 2 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  SPLIT <r>-data AT ',' INTO TABLE lt_cols.
  IF lt_cols[ 5 ]-table_line CA '-'. INSERT 'YYYY-MM-DD' INTO TABLE lt_date_fmts. ENDIF.
  IF lt_cols[ 5 ]-table_line CA '/'. INSERT 'MM/DD/YYYY' INTO TABLE lt_date_fmts. ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.
SORT lt_date_fmts. DELETE ADJACENT DUPLICATES FROM lt_date_fmts.
WRITE: / |Date format variants: { lines( lt_date_fmts ) }|.
